questions.question: What datasets did 'An Image is Worth 16x16 Words: Transformers for Image Recognition at Scale' use?
questions.gold_answer: ImageNet, ImageNet-21k, CIFAR-100
questions.system_answer: The paper reports pre-training on ImageNet, ImageNet-21k, and JFT-300M. It also evaluates transfer performance on smaller benchmarks including ImageNet, ImageNet-Real, CIFAR-100, and the VTAB suite of 19 tasks.

questions.question: What is the parameter count of the largest variant in 'Training data-efficient image transformers & distillation through attention'?
questions.system_answer: The largest variant listed in the evidence is ViT-L, with 307M parameters. If you mean the largest DeiT variant in this paper, the evidence shows DeiT-B★ at 87M parameters.

questions.question: What architectural innovations does 'Swin Transformer: Hierarchical Vision Transformer using Shifted Windows' introduce?
questions.gold_answer: hierarchical structure, shifted window attention
questions.system_answer: The paper introduces a hierarchical vision Transformer that computes self-attention inside non-overlapping local windows, then alternates with shifted window partitioning to create cross-window connections. It also uses patch merging to build multi-scale feature maps, and this design yields linear computational complexity with respect to image size. Relative position bias is another architectural choice highlighted as beneficial.

questions.question: Briefly describe the architecture of 'Emerging Properties in Self-Supervised Vision Transformers'.
questions.gold_answer: DINO is a self-supervised vision transformer training method
questions.system_answer: The paper presents DINO, a simple self-supervised architecture based on self-distillation without labels: a student network is trained to match a momentum-teacher network across multiple augmented views, using centering and sharpening to prevent collapse. It is designed to work with both Vision Transformers and convnets, but the paper emphasizes that ViTs with small patches benefit especially strongly.

questions.question: Do papers in this corpus agree on top-1 accuracy on ImageNet?
questions.gold_answer: No—reported top-1 ImageNet accuracy in this corpus varies widely from 1.3% to 91.0% across 594 results.
questions.system_answer: No. The corpus shows a very wide spread in reported ImageNet top-1 accuracy, from 1.3 to 91.0 (stddev 8.69), so the results are not consistent. There are also multiple conflicting SOTA claims with different values, e.g. CoCa (finetuned) at 91.0, PaLI-17B at 90.9, and MaxViT-XL reported at both 88.7 and 88.51.

questions.question: What is the range of top-1 accuracy values reported on CIFAR-100 in this corpus?
questions.gold_answer: The top-1 accuracy on CIFAR-100 in this corpus ranges from 48.5% to 91.88% across 27 results.

questions.question: Do papers in this corpus agree on which model holds SOTA on ADE20K mIoU?
questions.gold_answer: No—this corpus does not agree on a single ADE20K mIoU SOTA model, since 7 papers each claim SOTA on the benchmark.
questions.system_answer: No. The corpus does not fully agree: the highest ADE20K mIoU in the evidence is 62.9 for InternImage-H, but another paper also makes a strong SOTA claim with ViT-Adapter-L at 61.5, and SwinV2-G is listed at 59.9. So the papers disagree on which model is state-of-the-art, even though InternImage-H has the top reported value in this corpus.

questions.question: Do papers in this corpus agree on mIoU values on ADE20K?
questions.gold_answer: No—reported ADE20K mIoU values vary widely across 97 results, from 26.6 to 62.9.
questions.system_answer: No. The ADE20K mIoU values in this corpus vary widely, from 26.6 to 62.9 (spread 36.3, stddev 7.29), so they are not consistent. The highest reported value is 62.9 by InternImage-H, while other SOTA claims in the corpus include 61.5 for ViT-Adapter-L and 59.9 for SwinV2-G, indicating multiple differing SOTA numbers rather than a single consistent value.

questions.question: Do papers in this corpus agree on whether position embeddings are necessary for vision transformers?
questions.gold_answer: Papers disagree on the necessity of position embeddings; some ablations show minimal impact while others demonstrate clear performance drops without them.
questions.system_answer: The corpus does not show a direct disagreement about whether position embeddings are needed: the evidence consistently says vision transformers use positional information because self-attention is permutation-equivariant, and one paper proposes a new way to provide it. For example, one paper says positional encodings are “commonly employed to incorporate the order of sequences,” while another proposes conditional positional encoding because previous encodings are “predefined and independent of input tokens.” The evidence does not contain a paper claiming position embeddings are unnecessary for vision transformers.

questions.question: How did the maximum reported top-1 accuracy on ImageNet change year over year in this corpus?
questions.system_answer: The maximum reported ImageNet top-1 accuracy rose from 85.2 in 2020 to 90.0 in 2021 and 91.0 in 2022, then declined to 87.8 in 2023 and 83.9 in 2024. Overall, the trend is up from 2020 to a peak in 2022, followed by a downward trend through 2024.

questions.question: How many papers were published each year in this corpus, and which year had the most?
questions.system_answer: The corpus has 5 papers in 2019, 7 in 2020, 49 in 2021, 21 in 2022, 12 in 2023, 4 in 2024, and 2 in 2025. The year with the most papers is 2021 with 49 papers.

questions.question: What is the trend in average model parameter count by year in this corpus?
questions.gold_answer: increasing over time
questions.system_answer: The average model parameter count rises sharply from 160.872M in 2020 to 290.974M in 2021, then to 780.582M in 2022, and peaks at 4,098.764M in 2023. It then drops steeply to 50.0M in 2024, so the overall trend is up through 2023 and then down in 2024.

questions.question: In which year did papers in this corpus first mention self-supervised learning?
questions.system_answer: The first paper in the corpus that mentions self-supervised learning appears in 2020. The trend starts at 1 mention in 2020, rises to 4 in 2021, then drops back to 1 in 2022 and stays flat at 1 in 2023.

questions.question: How did the number of papers using COCO change from year to year in this corpus?
questions.gold_answer: growing over time
questions.system_answer: The number of papers mentioning COCO fluctuated over time rather than changing steadily. It started at 4 in 2019, dropped to 1 in 2020, rose sharply to 17 in 2021, then declined to 10 in 2022, 4 in 2023, and 1 in 2024.
